title: The Unit Circle
objectives: Define sine and cosine using the unit circle
Find the coordinates of points on the unit circle for special angles
Memorize the unit circle values for multiples of $30¬į$ and $45¬į$
Use reference angles to find trigonometric values in any quadrant
Determine the sign of trigonometric functions by quadrant
Evaluate trigonometric functions exactly without a calculator

devotional.title: The Circle of God's Sovereignty
devotional.content:
Isaiah 40:22 tells us that God "sits enthroned above the circle of the 
earth." The ancient Hebrew conception of the Earth as circular (or 
disk-shaped) has been debated, but what's beyond debate is the theological 
point: God's perspective encompasses all.

The unit circle provides a striking mathematical image of this truth. 
From the center of the circle, every direction is visible. Every angle 
is accounted for. Nothing is hidden from the center's view.

As you learn the unit circle, you're not just memorizing coordinates. 
You're learning how every angle‚ÄĒno matter how large, how negative, or 
how far removed from the "nice" angles of the first quadrant‚ÄĒcan be 
understood in terms of a simple reference angle. Complexity reduces to 
simplicity. The exotic becomes familiar.

This mirrors how God sees our lives. What seems chaotic and unpredictable 
to us‚ÄĒthe angles we don't choose, the rotations that spin us around‚ÄĒis 
perfectly understood from His perspective at the center. He doesn't need 
reference angles; He sees the pattern directly.

"For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways," 
says the LORD in Isaiah 55:8. Our understanding of life is like working 
with reference angles‚ÄĒwe understand things by reducing them to the 
simple cases we know. God's understanding is complete, direct, and 
unmediated by human limitations.

Trust the One who sits at the center of all circles.

epigraph.text: *"He sits enthroned above the circle of the earth."*
epigraph.quote: The circle is the synthesis of the greatest oppositions.

content.text:
In previous courses, you likely defined trigonometric functions using 
right triangles‚ÄĒSOHCAHTOA and all that. But right triangles have a 
serious limitation: they only work for acute angles (angles between $0¬į$ 
and $90¬į$). What does "opposite over hypotenuse" even mean for an angle 
of $150¬į$? Or $-45¬į$? Or $720¬į$?

The unit circle solves this problem beautifully. By defining sine and 
cosine as coordinates on a circle, we extend trigonometry to *all* 
angles‚ÄĒpositive or negative, small or large, acute or not. This is the 
foundation for everything that follows in trigonometry.
The Unit Circle

content.title: Unit Circle
content.content:
The **unit circle** is the circle of radius 1 
centered at the origin. Its equation is:
$$
 x^2 + y^2 = 1
$$

content.text:
Why radius 1? Because it eliminates the need to divide by the radius when 
computing trigonometric values. The coordinates themselves become the 
trigonometric values.

content.title: Sine and Cosine via the Unit Circle
content.content:
For an angle $\theta$ in standard position, let $P$ be the point where 
the terminal side intersects the unit circle. If $P = (x, y)$, then:
$$
 \cos\theta = x \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sin\theta = y
$$
In other words: $P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.

content.caption: The unit circle definition of sine and cosine. For any angle $\theta$ in standard position, the terminal side intersects the unit circle at point $P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The $x$-coordinate is $\cos\theta$ and the $y$-coordinate is $\sin\theta$.
content.alt: The unit circle definition of sine and cosine. For any angle $\theta$ in standard position, the terminal side intersects the unit circle at point $P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The $x$-coordinate is $\cos\theta$ and the $y$-coordinate is $\sin\theta$.

content.text:
This definition has remarkable consequences. Since every point on the 
unit circle satisfies $x^2 + y^2 = 1$, we immediately get the most 
fundamental identity in trigonometry:

Also, since a point on the unit circle must satisfy $-1 \le x \le 1$ and 
$-1 \le y \le 1$:

content.title: Finding coordinates on the unit circle
content.problem:
The terminal side of angle $\theta$ in standard position passes through 
the point $(0.6, 0.8)$ on the unit circle. Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.
content.solution:
Since $(0.6, 0.8)$ is on the unit circle and lies on the terminal side 
of $\theta$:
$$
 \cos\theta = 0.6 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sin\theta = 0.8
$$

We can verify this is on the unit circle: $0.6^2 + 0.8^2 = 0.36 + 0.64 = 1$. ‚úď

content.text: The Quadrantal Angles

The simplest angles are those whose terminal sides lie along the axes. 
For these **quadrantal angles**, we can 
read the coordinates directly:

content.title: Evaluating quadrantal angles
content.problem:
Evaluate each without a calculator:

1. $\cos(\pi)$

2. $\sin\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right)$

3. $\sin(0)$

4. $\cos(-\pi)$
content.solution:
1. At $\theta = \pi$, the point is $(-1, 0)$, so $\cos(\pi) = -1$.

2. At $\theta = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$, the point is $(0, -1)$, so 
 $\sin\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right) = -1$.

3. At $\theta = 0$, the point is $(1, 0)$, so $\sin(0) = 0$.

4. The angle $-\pi$ is coterminal with $\pi$ (same terminal side), 
 so $\cos(-\pi) = \cos(\pi) = -1$.

content.text: Special Angles in the First Quadrant

Beyond the quadrantal angles, there are three special angles whose 
trigonometric values can be found exactly using geometry. These come 
from the 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangles you studied in geometry.
The 45¬į Angle ($\pi/4$ radians)

Consider an isoceles right triangle with legs of length 1. By the 
Pythagorean theorem, the hypotenuse is $\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$.

If we scale this triangle so the hypotenuse equals 1 (to fit on the 
unit circle), we divide all sides by $\sqrt{2}$:
$$
 \text{legs} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
$$

So the point on the unit circle at $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ (or $45¬į$) is:
$$
 \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)
$$
Therefore: $\cos\dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\sin\dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
The 30¬į and 60¬į Angles ($\pi/6$ and $\pi/3$ radians)

Start with an equilateral triangle with sides of length 2. Cutting it 
in half creates a 30-60-90 triangle with:
- Short leg: 1 (half the base)
- Hypotenuse: 2 (a side of the original triangle)
- Long leg: $\sqrt{2^2 - 1^2} = \sqrt{3}$ (by Pythagorean theorem)

Scaling so the hypotenuse equals 1 (dividing all sides by 2):
$$
 \text{short leg} = \frac{1}{2} \qquad \text{long leg} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

For $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ (30¬į), the adjacent side (horizontal) is the 
long leg and the opposite side (vertical) is the short leg:
$$
 \cos\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \qquad \sin\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

For $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ (60¬į), the roles are reversed:
$$
 \cos\frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{1}{2} \qquad \sin\frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$
Summary: Special Angles in Quadrant I

content.title: The Square Root Pattern
content.content:
Here is a powerful way to memorize the first-quadrant values. Notice that 
all the values are of the form $\dfrac{\sqrt{n}}{2}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**For sine, count UP from 0 to 4:**
$$
\sin 0¬į = 0, \quad \sin 30¬į = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad \sin 45¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \quad \sin 60¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad \sin 90¬į = 1
$$

**For cosine, count DOWN from 4 to 0:**
$$
\cos 0¬į = 1, \quad \cos 30¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad \cos 45¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \quad \cos 60¬į = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad \cos 90¬į = 0
$$

**Memory aid:** "0-1-2-3-4 for sine (going up), 4-3-2-1-0 for cosine (going down)."

This pattern also explains *why* sine and cosine are complementary: 
$\sin\theta = \cos(90¬į - \theta)$. When sine counts up to $n$, cosine 
counts down from $4-n$ to the same value!

Students often confuse $\sin 30¬į$ and $\sin 60¬į$. Remember: sine gives 
the *height* ($y$-coordinate). A 30¬į angle is less steep than 60¬į, 
so its height is smaller. Thus $\sin 30¬į = \frac{1}{2}$ (smaller) and 
$\sin 60¬į = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ (larger).

content.text: Reference Angles

The special angles we've memorized are all in the first quadrant. How 
do we find values for angles in other quadrants?

content.title: Reference Angle
content.content:
The **reference angle** for an angle $\theta$ 
in standard position is the acute angle formed between the terminal side 
of $\theta$ and the $x$-axis. It is always positive and between $0$ and 
$\frac{\pi}{2}$ (or between $0¬į$ and $90¬į$).

content.title: Finding Reference Angles
content.content:
For an angle $\theta$ in standard position, the reference angle $\hat{\theta}$ is:

- **Quadrant I:** $\hat{\theta} = \theta$

- **Quadrant II:** $\hat{\theta} = \pi - \theta$ (or $180¬į - \theta$)

- **Quadrant III:** $\hat{\theta} = \theta - \pi$ (or $\theta - 180¬į$)

- **Quadrant IV:** $\hat{\theta} = 2\pi - \theta$ (or $360¬į - \theta$)

content.title: Finding Reference Angles
content.content:
Given any angle $\theta$, follow this flowchart to find its reference angle $\hat{\theta}$:

**Quick quadrant check (for angles in $[0, 2\pi)$):**

- QI: $0 \le \theta < \frac{\pi}{2}$ (or $0¬į \le \theta < 90¬į$)

- QII: $\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi$ (or $90¬į < \theta < 180¬į$)

- QIII: $\pi < \theta < \frac{3\pi}{2}$ (or $180¬į < \theta < 270¬į$)

- QIV: $\frac{3\pi}{2} < \theta < 2\pi$ (or $270¬į < \theta < 360¬į$)

**Memory aid:** The reference angle always measures from the terminal 
side to the *nearest* part of the $x$-axis (not the $y$-axis!).

content.title: Finding reference angles
content.problem:
Find the reference angle for each:

1. $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

2. $225¬į$

3. $\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

4. $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$
content.solution:
1. $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ is in Quadrant II (between $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $\pi$).
 $$
 \hat{\theta} = \pi - \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{6\pi}{6} - \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}
 $$

2. $225¬į$ is in Quadrant III (between $180¬į$ and $270¬į$).
 $$
 \hat{\theta} = 225¬į - 180¬į = 45¬į
 $$

3. $\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$ is in Quadrant IV (between $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ and $2\pi$).
 $$
 \hat{\theta} = 2\pi - \frac{5\pi}{3} = \frac{6\pi}{3} - \frac{5\pi}{3} = \frac{\pi}{3}
 $$

4. $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ is in Quadrant IV (clockwise from the positive $x$-axis).
 $$
 \hat{\theta} = \left|-\frac{\pi}{4}\right| = \frac{\pi}{4}
 $$
 (Or note that $-\frac{\pi}{4}$ is coterminal with $\frac{7\pi}{4}$, which 
 is in QIV with reference angle $2\pi - \frac{7\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}$.)

content.text: Signs in Each Quadrant

The reference angle tells us the *magnitude* of sine and cosine. 
To determine the *sign*, we need to know which quadrant the angle 
is in. This comes directly from the unit circle definition:
- $\cos\theta = x$, so cosine is positive when $x > 0$ (right side)
- $\sin\theta = y$, so sine is positive when $y > 0$ (upper half)
Even and Odd Trigonometric Functions

The unit circle reveals an important symmetry. Consider an angle $\theta$ 
and its negative $-\theta$. On the unit circle, $-\theta$ is the reflection 
of $\theta$ across the $x$-axis.

If the point for $\theta$ is $(x, y)$, then the point for $-\theta$ is 
$(x, -y)$. Since $\cos\theta = x$ and $\sin\theta = y$:

content.title: Using even-odd properties
content.problem:
Use the even-odd properties to evaluate:

1. $\cos(-60¬į)$

2. $\sin\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$

3. $\cos\left(-\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\right)$
content.solution:
1. Since cosine is even: $\cos(-60¬į) = \cos(60¬į) = \dfrac{1}{2}$

2. Since sine is odd: $\sin\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = -\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

3. Since cosine is even: $\cos\left(-\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\right) = \cos\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\right) = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
 
 (Note: $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ is in QII where cosine is negative.)

content.text: Periodic Functions

As an angle increases, the point on the unit circle travels around and 
eventually returns to where it started. After one complete revolution 
($2\pi$ radians or $360¬į$), the angle has the same terminal point as 
the original. This means the trigonometric values repeat.

content.title: Periodic Function
content.content:
A function $f$ is **periodic** if there exists a positive number $p$ 
such that $f(x + p) = f(x)$ for all $x$ in the domain. The smallest such 
$p$ is called the **period** of the function.

content.caption: Periodicity: The angles $\theta$ and $\theta + 2\pi$ have the same terminal point $P$ on the unit circle, so $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$ and $\cos(\theta + 2\pi) = \cos\theta$.
content.alt: Periodicity: The angles $\theta$ and $\theta + 2\pi$ have the same terminal point $P$ on the unit circle, so $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$ and $\cos(\theta + 2\pi) = \cos\theta$.

content.title: Period of Sine and Cosine
content.content:
Sine and cosine are periodic with period $2\pi$:
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin(\theta + 2\pi) &= \sin\theta \\
 \cos(\theta + 2\pi) &= \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$
More generally, for any integer $n$:
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin(\theta + 2\pi n) &= \sin\theta \\
 \cos(\theta + 2\pi n) &= \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Using periodicity
content.problem:
Evaluate using periodicity:

1. $\sin\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{6}\right)$

2. $\cos(840¬į)$

content.title: Domain of Sine and Cosine
content.content:
The domain of both $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ is all real numbers: 
$(-\infty, \infty)$.

The range of both functions is $[-1, 1]$.

content.text: Evaluating Using Reference Angles
Now we can find exact values for any angle that has a special reference angle.

content.title: Using Reference Angles
content.content:
The sine and cosine of any angle equal (in absolute value) the sine 
and cosine of its reference angle. The sign is determined by the quadrant:
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\theta &= \pm\sin\hat{\theta} \\
 \cos\theta &= \pm\cos\hat{\theta}
\end{aligned}$$
where the sign is positive if that function is positive in $\theta$'s 
quadrant, negative otherwise.

content.title: Evaluating using reference angles
content.problem:
Find the exact value of each:

1. $\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

2. $\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

3. $\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

4. $\cos\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$
content.solution:
1. $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ is in QII with reference angle $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.
 
 In QII, sine is positive.
 $$
 \sin\frac{5\pi}{6} = +\sin\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}
 $$

2. $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ is in QII. In QII, cosine is negative.
 $$
 \cos\frac{5\pi}{6} = -\cos\frac{\pi}{6} = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
 $$

3. $\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$ is in QIII with reference angle 
 $\dfrac{5\pi}{4} - \pi = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
 
 In QIII, sine is negative.
 $$
 \sin\frac{5\pi}{4} = -\sin\frac{\pi}{4} = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
 $$

4. $\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$ is in QIV with reference angle 
 $2\pi - \dfrac{11\pi}{6} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.
 
 In QIV, cosine is positive.
 $$
 \cos\frac{11\pi}{6} = +\cos\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
 $$

content.title: Complete evaluation
content.problem: Find all six trigonometric values for $\theta = \dfrac{4\pi}{3}$.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Identify quadrant and reference angle.**

$\dfrac{4\pi}{3} = \dfrac{4 \times 180¬į}{3} = 240¬į$, which is in QIII.

Reference angle: $\dfrac{4\pi}{3} - \pi = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ (or $240¬į - 180¬į = 60¬į$).

**Step 2: Find sine and cosine.**

In QIII, both sine and cosine are negative.
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\frac{4\pi}{3} &= -\sin\frac{\pi}{3} = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\
 \cos\frac{4\pi}{3} &= -\cos\frac{\pi}{3} = -\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Find the other functions.**
$$\begin{aligned}
 \tan\frac{4\pi}{3} &= \frac{\sin(4\pi/3)}{\cos(4\pi/3)} = \frac{-\sqrt{3}/2}{-1/2} = \sqrt{3} \\[4pt]
 \csc\frac{4\pi}{3} &= \frac{1}{\sin(4\pi/3)} = \frac{1}{-\sqrt{3}/2} = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \\[4pt]
 \sec\frac{4\pi}{3} &= \frac{1}{\cos(4\pi/3)} = \frac{1}{-1/2} = -2 \\[4pt]
 \cot\frac{4\pi}{3} &= \frac{1}{\tan(4\pi/3)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

content.text: Finding an Angle Given a Trigonometric Value

Sometimes we need to work backward: given a trigonometric value and some 
information about the angle, find the angle.

content.title: Finding an angle from a trig value
content.problem: If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi$, find $\theta$.
content.solution:
The condition $\dfrac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi$ tells us $\theta$ is in QII.

We need an angle in QII whose sine is $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

The reference angle with $\sin\hat{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ is $\hat{\theta} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

For a QII angle: $\theta = \pi - \hat{\theta} = \pi - \dfrac{\pi}{6} = \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$.

content.problem:
[Finding all angles in $[0, 2\pi)$]

Find all angles $\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi)$ such that $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
content.solution:
First, find the reference angle. We need $\cos\hat{\theta} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, 
which gives $\hat{\theta} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

Now, cosine is negative in QII and QIII.

**QII:** $\theta = \pi - \dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

**QIII:** $\theta = \pi + \dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

The solutions are $\theta = \dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ and $\theta = \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$.

content.text: The Complete Unit Circle

Here is the unit circle with all the special angles. This diagram should 
eventually become second nature‚ÄĒyou should be able to reconstruct it 
from memory.

content.caption: The complete unit circle showing all 16 special angles with their coordinates. Each point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ can be determined from the first quadrant values using reference angles and quadrant signs.
content.alt: The complete unit circle showing all 16 special angles with their coordinates. Each point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ can be determined from the first quadrant values using reference angles and quadrant signs.

content.title: The Unit Circle
content.content:
- The **unit circle** is $x^2 + y^2 = 1$. For angle $\theta$, 
 the terminal point is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.

- **Pythagorean Identity:** $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$

- **Special angles** to memorize (first quadrant):
 | $\theta$ | $0$ | $\frac{\pi}{6}$ | $\frac{\pi}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi}{3}$ | $\frac{\pi}{2}$ |
| $\cos\theta$ | $1$ | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ | $\frac{1}{2}$ | $0$ |
| $\sin\theta$ | $0$ | $\frac{1}{2}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ | $1$ |

 - The **reference angle** is the acute angle to the $x$-axis.

- **Signs by quadrant:** All positive in QI; Sine in QII; 
 Tangent in QIII; Cosine in QIV ("All Students Take Calculus").

- **Even-Odd:** $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$ (even); 
 $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$ (odd).

- **Periodicity:** $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$ and 
 $\cos(\theta + 2\pi) = \cos\theta$. Period is $2\pi$.

- **Domain:** All real numbers. **Range:** $[-1, 1]$.

content.problem:
Fill in the blank with the correct term.

1. The __________ is the circle of radius 1 centered at the origin.

2. For an angle $\theta$ in standard position, the coordinates of the point where the terminal side meets the unit circle are $(\_\_\_\_\_, \_\_\_\_\_)$.

3. The acute angle formed between the terminal side and the $x$-axis is called the __________ angle.

4. The identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ is called the __________ Identity.

5. A function $f$ is __________ if $f(-x) = f(x)$ for all $x$ in its domain.

6. A function $f$ is __________ if $f(-x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$ in its domain.

7. A function is __________ if its values repeat at regular intervals.

True or False? Justify your answer.

1. $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ for all $\theta$

2. $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$ for all $\theta$

3. $\cos(-\theta) = -\cos\theta$ for all $\theta$

4. The reference angle is always between $0$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

5. $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$ for all $\theta$

6. The point $(0.6, 0.8)$ is on the unit circle.

Determine if the point is on the unit circle. Show your verification.

1. $\left(\dfrac{3}{5}, \dfrac{4}{5}\right)$

2. $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$

3. $\left(\dfrac{5}{13}, \dfrac{12}{13}\right)$

4. $(0.6, 0.8)$

5. $\left(\dfrac{1}{3}, \dfrac{2}{3}\right)$

6. $\left(-\dfrac{7}{25}, \dfrac{24}{25}\right)$

Find the missing coordinate for a point on the unit circle.

1. $\left(\dfrac{3}{5}, y\right)$, $y > 0$

2. $(x, \dfrac{5}{13})$, $x < 0$

3. $\left(-\dfrac{7}{25}, y\right)$, $y < 0$

4. $\left(x, -\dfrac{2}{3}\right)$, $x > 0$

Find the coordinates of the point on the unit circle corresponding to each angle.

1. $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ (b) $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ (c) $\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$ 
 (d) $-\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ (e) $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$ (f) $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Determine the quadrant in which $\theta$ lies.

1. $\theta = \dfrac{5\pi}{8}$ (b) $\theta = \dfrac{7\pi}{5}$ 
 (c) $\theta = -\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ (d) $\theta = 5.2$ radians

Find the reference angle for each angle in degrees.

1. $120¬į$

2. $135¬į$

3. $150¬į$

4. $200¬į$

5. $225¬į$

6. $240¬į$

7. $300¬į$

8. $330¬į$

Find the reference angle for each angle in radians.

1. $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

2. $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

3. $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

4. $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$

5. $\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

6. $\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

7. $\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

8. $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

Find the reference angle for each (negative or large angles).

1. $-45¬į$

2. $-120¬į$

3. $400¬į$

4. $-200¬į$

5. $\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$

6. $-\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

7. $\dfrac{13\pi}{6}$

8. $-\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

Find the exact value at standard first-quadrant angles.

1. $\sin 0$

2. $\cos 0$

3. $\sin \dfrac{\pi}{6}$

4. $\cos \dfrac{\pi}{6}$

5. $\sin \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

6. $\cos \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

7. $\sin \dfrac{\pi}{3}$

8. $\cos \dfrac{\pi}{3}$

9. $\sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

10. $\cos \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

11. $\sin \pi$

12. $\cos \pi$

Find the exact value using reference angles (Quadrants II and III).

1. $\sin \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

2. $\cos \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

3. $\sin \dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

4. $\cos \dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

5. $\sin \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

6. $\cos \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

7. $\sin \dfrac{7\pi}{6}$

8. $\cos \dfrac{7\pi}{6}$

9. $\sin \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

10. $\cos \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

11. $\sin \dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

12. $\cos \dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

Find the exact value using reference angles (Quadrant IV and beyond).

1. $\sin \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$

2. $\cos \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$

3. $\sin \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

4. $\cos \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

5. $\sin \dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

6. $\cos \dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

The displacement from equilibrium of an oscillating weight suspended by a 
spring is given by
$$
 y(t) = \frac{1}{2}\cos(6t)
$$
where $y$ is the displacement in feet and $t$ is the time in seconds. Find 
the displacement when:

1. $t = 0$

2. $t = \dfrac{\pi}{12}$

3. $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$

The horizontal displacement of a pendulum from its rest position is modeled by
$$
 x(t) = 4\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{2}\right)
$$
where $x$ is in centimeters and $t$ is in seconds. Find the displacement when:

1. $t = 0$

2. $t = 1$

3. $t = 2$

4. $t = 3$

A point on the rim of a wheel of radius 3 feet rotates at constant angular 
velocity. Its height above the center is given by $y(t) = 3\sin(\omega t)$ 
where $\omega$ is the angular velocity. If the wheel completes one revolution 
in $2\pi$ seconds (so $\omega = 1$), find the height when:

1. $t = 0$

2. $t = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

3. $t = \pi$

4. $t = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$
Explain why $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ must be true for all $\theta$.

If $\sin\theta = 0.8$, what are the possible values of $\cos\theta$? 
Explain why there are two possibilities.

Why is the point $(0.6, 0.9)$ NOT on the unit circle? If an angle $\theta$ 
has terminal point on the line through the origin and $(0.6, 0.9)$, what 
point on the unit circle corresponds to $\theta$?

Explain why:

1. $-1 \le \sin\theta \le 1$ for all $\theta$

2. $\sin\theta$ and $\csc\theta$ always have the same sign

3. If $\theta$ is in Quadrant II, then $\sin\theta > 0$ and $\cos\theta < 0$
Complete the statement: "If $\sin\theta > 0$ and $\tan\theta < 0$, then $\theta$ is in Quadrant ___." Explain your reasoning.

How many solutions does $\sin\theta = 0.5$ have in:

1. $[0, 2\pi)$

2. $[0, 4\pi)$

3. $(-\infty, \infty)$

*{Visual Interpretation

Use the figure below showing an angle $\theta$ in standard position with 
terminal point $P$ on the unit circle in Quadrant III.

1. Are all six trigonometric functions of $\theta$ defined? Explain.

2. For each trigonometric function that is defined, is it positive or negative? Explain.

3. Is $\sin\theta$ greater than or less than $\cos\theta$? Explain using the coordinates of $P$.

The figure below shows two angles, $\alpha$ and $\beta$, in standard position 
on the unit circle.

1. Based on the figure, what appears to be the relationship between 
 $\sin\alpha$ and $\sin\beta$?

2. What is the relationship between $\cos\alpha$ and $\cos\beta$?

3. If $\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$, what is $\beta$?

4. Make a conjecture: For any angle $\alpha$ in QI, if $\beta = \pi - \alpha$, 
 then $\sin\beta = \_\_\_$ and $\cos\beta = \_\_\_$.
Use a calculator to verify that $\sin^2 35¬į + \cos^2 35¬į = 1$.
Use a calculator to find $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$, and $\tan\theta$ for $\theta = 1.2$ radians. Verify that $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$.
Create a table of values for $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ at $\theta = 0¬į, 10¬į, 20¬į, \ldots, 90¬į$. What patterns do you notice?

Graph $y = \sin x$ and $y = \cos x$ on the same axes for $0 \le x \le 2\pi$. At what points do they intersect?

*{Error Analysis

Describe and correct the error.

Finding $\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$:
> "Reference angle is $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$. $\cos\dfrac{\pi}{6} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

So $\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{6} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$." ‚úó

Describe and correct the error.

Finding $\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$:
> "$\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$ is in Quadrant IV. Reference angle is $\dfrac{5\pi}{3} - \pi = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.

$\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{3} = -\sin\dfrac{2\pi}{3} = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$" ‚úó

Describe and correct the error.

Finding all values when $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in QII:
> "$\sin^2\theta = 1 - \dfrac{9}{25} = \dfrac{16}{25}$

$\sin\theta = \dfrac{4}{5}$ or $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{4}{5}$

So there are two possible values for sine." ‚úó
*{Challenge Problems

The terminal side of angle $\theta$ passes through the point $(3, -4)$ 
(not on the unit circle). Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.
The terminal side of angle $\theta$ passes through the point $(a, a)$ where $a > 0$. Find all six trigonometric values of $\theta$.
If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ and $\theta$ is in QII, find $\cos\theta$, $\tan\theta$, $\cot\theta$, $\sec\theta$, $\csc\theta$ exactly.
Prove that for any angle $\theta$, $|\sin\theta| \le 1$ and $|\cos\theta| \le 1$.
Prove that $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ using the unit circle definition.

If $\sin\theta = k$ for some value $k$ with $-1 < k < 1$, how many 
solutions are there in the interval $[0, 2\pi)$? What if $k = 1$ or $k = -1$?
Find all angles $\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi)$ such that $2\sin^2\theta - \sin\theta - 1 = 0$.

If $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{3}{5}$ with $\alpha$ in QI, and $\cos\beta = -\dfrac{12}{13}$ with $\beta$ in QII, find $\sin(\alpha + \beta)$ using the identity $\sin(\alpha + \beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta$.
Show that $\sin(90¬į - \theta) = \cos\theta$ for any acute angle $\theta$ by using the unit circle.
Prove that for any point $(x, y)$ on the unit circle, there exists an angle $\theta$ such that $x = \cos\theta$ and $y = \sin\theta$.

marginNotes.text:
I was there when he set the heavens in 
place, when he marked out the horizon on the face of the deep.
